text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to be talking about robotic ventral hernia repair in patients with morbid obesity.
This is a forty-year-old woman, previous laparoscopic hysterectomy with an umbilical extraction and developed a symptomatic hernia. She had a BMI of forty-two with lifelong attempts at weight loss.
We enrolled her in our pre-op optimization program. She was otherwise optimized but could not lose weight and was very limited by this hernia.

Pre-op optimization is something we really talked a lot about in hernia surgery. Diabetes control, weight loss, and smoking cessation are really important elements to this, a lot of other opportunities for optimization.
Our goal is to offset the effects of a surgical insult and enhance physical conditioning, reverse deconditioning, and increase functional reserve.

This woman actually did really well with activity cardiopulmonary rehabilitation but just could not lose weight there's some recent literature highlighting that robotic surgery can be safe in patients with morbid obesity and bmis of greater than forty i reviewed her imaging she had a six by twelve centimeter incisional hernia

I felt that she had a very good likelihood of being an exclusive robotic abdominal wall reconstruction, and we could reduce the potential for wound morbidity associated with open surgery. So I planned for an ETEP approach to address her ventral incisional hernia.
I planned for a left retromuscular lateral doc with a plan for a retro rectus repair.
The surgeon initiates ETEP access using a KII Fios optical entry trocar, which allows concurrent insufflation during entry into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon presents a retromuscular view after pneumodissection and blunt dissection, showing the placement of trocars and the cleared retrorectus space. A thorough dissection of the patient's left retromuscular space is completed before docking the robotic system.

After docking the robotic system, the surgeon prepares to perform a crossover technique in the upper abdomen, which remains relatively untouched. The presence of a diastasis is noted, indicating the need for a high placation.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath one centimeter lateral to the linea alba, exposing a cuff of fascia for easier closure later. A large preperitoneal fat pocket is visible, facilitating the retro muscular repair and simplifying the crossover procedure.

The surgeon identifies the contralateral rectus muscle and incises the posterior sheath, entering the retro-rectus space. The incision is made precisely to avoid cutting into the linea alba, ensuring no iatrogenic injuries occur.

The surgeon continues to develop the retrorectus dissection after exposing and incising the posterior sheath. This step is performed carefully to avoid entering the hernia sac, which could compromise visualization.
The surgeon enters the peri-umbilical hernia sac during the procedure, with visualization remaining clear due to the extensive retromuscular dissection previously performed.
The surgeon identifies a hernia containing omentum and begins dissecting it out. The multifocal defect is visible, and the omentum is carefully reduced from the hernia sac.

The surgeon carefully maintains the integrity of the posterior sheath, hernia sac, and peritoneum during the dissection, aiming to facilitate easier visceral sac reconstruction. The dissection progresses further down the surgical field.

The surgeon reaches six to seven centimeters below the inferior aspect of the hernia and proceeds with completing the retro muscular dissection on the contralateral side, carefully removing all areolar tissue.

The surgeon carefully stays close to the posterior sheath, reflecting neurovascular elements towards the anterior abdominal wall. The hernia and diastasis are visible. The superior dissection is extended by two to four centimeters, followed by anterior fascial closure.

The surgeon performs plication of the midline both below and above the hernia defect for approximately five to seven centimeters, re-approximating the muscle bellies. A number one non-absorbable suture is used to close the hernia defect, visibly bringing the tissue together with minimal tension.

The surgeon completes the sequential tightening of sutures, successfully re-approximating the midline and reconstructing the linea alba. The fascia visibly approximates without issue under intra-abdominal pressure of around ten.
The surgeon measures the retromuscular space and closes the posterior defect using a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture. The macroporous polypropylene mesh is then strategically measured for placement.

The surgeon measures and rolls out a large macroporous polypropylene mesh, approximately twenty-three by twenty-six centimeters, ensuring it lays flat within the surgical field. Tissio adhesive is applied, and a retromuscular drain is placed for fluid management.

So that's the case, uncomplicated post-op course, same-day discharge. I think robotic approaches may improve our ability to provide safe and durable hernia repair in patients with risk factors for perioperative soft tissue complications like her. I hope you enjoyed it. Thank you.
